title: Sellou
subtitle: Moroccan toasted flour confection with sesame, almonds, honey and spices
excerpt: Sellou is a Moroccan confection made by toasting plain flour in a dry pan until it smells nutty and turns light golden, then combining it with toasted sesame seeds, toasted almonds, honey, butter, anise and cinnamon. The mixture is pressed into moulds or shaped into balls. It is eaten by the spoonful or crumbled over bread. It is particularly associated with Ramadan and postpartum recovery. No oven is required. The whole process takes about 45 minutes.
sourceNotes: Sellou (also known as sfouf or zamita in Algeria) is documented in Paula Wolfert's 'Couscous and Other Good Food from Morocco' (1973) and Anissa Helou's 'Feast: Food of the Islamic World' (2018). The confection is made in large batches for Ramadan and for new mothers, who are traditionally fed it for the nutrients provided by the honey, almonds and sesame. The recipe here follows the Moroccan home kitchen tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Sellou keeps in an airtight tin at room temperature for up to 3 weeks. The flavour deepens after the first day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven required; all toasting done on the stovetop over medium-low heat

glossaryTerms.definition: A category of sweet where raw flour is dry-toasted in a pan until it turns light golden and smells nutty, then combined with fats, sweeteners and other toasted ingredients to make a crumbly, cohesive mixture. Toasting the flour drives off moisture, gelatinises some of the starch and develops complex, nutty flavour compounds through the Maillard reaction. The toasted flour behaves differently from raw flour: it absorbs fat rather than water, and it does not form gluten, which is why the finished confection is sandy and crumbly rather than doughy. The process requires constant stirring and medium-low heat to avoid scorching.

content.text: The key to sellou is patience:

slowly over medium-low heat on the hob, stirring all the time, until it turns light golden and smells nutty. This takes about 15 minutes. Rushing with higher heat scorches the flour and makes the sellou bitter.

The finished mixture should hold its shape when pressed into a ball or mould, but crumble at a touch. Add the butter a little at a time and stop when this texture is reached. The amount needed varies with the honey used and how dark the flour was toasted.

Spread the 300 g flour in a large dry frying pan over medium-low heat. Stir constantly with a wooden spoon for 12 to 15 minutes until the flour turns light golden brown and smells distinctly nutty. Do not let it darken to tan or brown; pale golden is what you want. Tip into a large mixing bowl.

In the same pan, toast the 150 g sesame seeds over medium heat for 3 to 4 minutes until golden and fragrant. Add to the flour. Toast the 150 g blanched almonds for 4 to 5 minutes until golden, then roughly chop or pulse briefly in a food processor until coarsely ground. Add to the bowl.

Add the 30 g icing sugar, 1 tsp ground anise and 1 tsp cinnamon to the bowl and mix well. Pour in the 150 g honey and mix. Add the 100 g melted butter a little at a time, mixing after each addition, until the mixture just holds together when pressed: it should clump rather than crumble, but should not be wet or greasy. Press into a shallow dish or mould, smoothing the surface with the back of a spoon, or roll into balls about 3 cm across. Leave to set for at least 30 minutes before serving.

items.problem: Sellou is too dry and powdery and will not press into shape
items.solution: Add more melted butter a tablespoon at a time, mixing thoroughly between additions, until the mixture clumps when pressed. Different flour toasting levels affect how much fat is needed.
The flour was toasted too dark. Pale gold is the right colour, not tan. Once the flour smells nutty it is close to done; remove it from the heat as soon as it turns light golden.

items.problem: Mixture is too soft and oily
items.solution: Too much butter was added. Spread the mixture out on a tray and leave at room temperature for 30 minutes; the butter will firm up as it cools. Next time, stop adding butter as soon as the mixture holds together when pressed.
